question: What is the Grace CPU's role in the GB200 system, and why is a CPU integrated alongside the Blackwell GPU in the same package?

findings: The GB200 Grace Blackwell Superchip combines two NVIDIA B200 Tensor Core GPUs with one NVIDIA Grace CPU via an ultra-low-power NVLink chip-to-chip interconnect running at 900 GB/s, and the chip-to-chip link is completely memory-coherent, creating a unified chip without memory localization.

Grace is designed specifically to feed the GPUs — its primary roles are to run the CUDA software scheduler, feed the GPUs, and expand memory and host additional high-speed I/O, rather than to serve as a general high-performance CPU.

GB200 packages two Blackwell GPUs per one Grace CPU (a 2:1 GPU-to-CPU ratio), which brings the platform near TCO parity with x86 systems and is far more compelling from a power, thermal, system, and networking integration perspective than the prior Grace-Hopper 1:1 approach.

The Grace interconnect fabric enables memory-coherent CPU-GPU communication; anyone trying to duplicate the GB200 NVL72's connectivity and coherency without NVLink will face massive power and TCO penalties.

The core of the GB200 is a multi-chip package containing two Blackwell GPUs and one Grace CPU linked by a 900 GB/s NVLink-C2C interconnect; each compute tray in an NVL72 rack holds two of these modules (2 Grace CPUs + 4 GPUs), and in total the NVL72 rack houses 36 modules (72 GPUs, 36 72-core CPUs) in 18 1U servers.
